food_subgroup: Self-selection fish-paste-stuffed tofu and vegetables in clear anchovy broth with bee hoon (rice vermicelli); hawker soup dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Yong Tau Foo soup as a prepared Singapore hawker dish. No fabricated code assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tofu and vegetable soup with fish paste stuffing (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Yong Tau Foo hawker dish)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0KDA used as closest defensible parent. Exact FoodEx2 code for Singapore Yong Tau Foo does not exist in the standard catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Yong Tau Foo as a prepared hawker dish.

main_ingredients: bitter gourd stuffed with fish paste
allergens: gluten (crab stick may contain wheat starch)

serving.description: 1 bowl (8 items + bee hoon + clear broth)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving consists of 8 selected items (~280g: 2 tofu pieces, 2 stuffed vegetables, 1 fish cake, 1 tofu skin roll, 1 tau pok, 1 crab stick), approximately 80g bee hoon (rice vermicelli), and ~300ml clear anchovy-vegetable broth, totalling approximately 720g.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo Clear Soup — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yong Tau Foo clear soup, cross-referenced with component-level calculation across the 8-item selection (tofu, tau pok, tofu skin roll, fish cake, crab stick, stuffed bitter gourd, stuffed capsicum) plus bee hoon and anchovy broth. Micronutrient values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component analogues where HPB data was not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice noodles and mixed fish-tofu dishes as analogous reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Yong Tau Foo clear soup. GI ~50 estimated using rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~53–58 per University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles), moderated downward by the high protein content from fish paste, tofu, and fish cake, and the fat content from tau pok and tofu skin. GL ~22 calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (50 × 48.2) / 100 ≈ 24, rounded to 22 per HPB component estimate. This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for precision.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~700mg per bowl from anchovy broth and fish paste items
hypertension.guidance: A standard 8-item bowl of Yong Tau Foo clear soup contributes approximately 700mg sodium (~30% of the 2,300mg daily limit), primarily from the anchovy broth (~350mg) and fish paste stuffing; patients with hypertension should request reduced broth volume or consume only half the broth to limit sodium intake to under 400mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load ~22 per bowl driven by ~48g carbohydrate from bee hoon (rice vermicelli)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 50 and a glycemic load of ~22 per bowl, Yong Tau Foo clear soup is one of the more T2DM-compatible hawker noodle dishes; patients with Type 2 Diabetes can further reduce glycemic impact by requesting a smaller bee hoon portion (~40g) or substituting with konjac noodles, and should pair the meal with the high-protein fish paste items to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~3.6g per bowl), moderate sodium, and high lean protein from fish paste and tofu
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yong Tau Foo clear soup is a heart-favourable hawker option with low saturated fat (~0.5g per 100g, ~3.6g per bowl), no trans fat, and lean protein from fish paste and tofu; patients with cardiovascular disease should choose blanched rather than fried items to minimise fat intake and consume only half the broth to limit sodium to under 400mg per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, soy isoflavones from tofu, and anti-inflammatory vegetable components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yong Tau Foo clear soup is suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its low saturated fat content, soy isoflavones from tofu (~15mg per 2 pieces with mild phytoestrogenic anti-inflammatory activity), and bitter gourd providing trace charantin; the dish supports an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern when fried items are avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (酿豆腐 / 釀豆腐) Clear Soup is a popular Singapore hawker dish of Hakka Chinese origin, served as a self-selection meal at nearly every hawker centre in Singapore and Malaysia. A standard bowl consists of 8 customer-selected items — typically 2 pieces of firm tofu, 2 stuffed vegetables (bitter gourd and capsicum filled with fish paste), 1 fish cake, 1 tofu skin roll, 1 tau pok (fried tofu puff), and 1 crab stick — served in a clear anchovy-vegetable broth (~300ml) with bee hoon (rice vermicelli, ~80g), totalling approximately 720g per bowl. The items are blanched in the broth; some items such as tau pok may be pre-fried. The dish has a mild, savoury flavour with a light, clean broth and a soft-to-firm texture across the tofu and fish paste items. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 54 kcal, 3.1g protein, 1.7g fat, 6.7g carbohydrate, 0.5g dietary fibre, and 97mg sodium. Per bowl (~720g): approximately 389 kcal, 22g protein, 12g fat, 48g carbohydrate, and 700mg sodium. GI is estimated at ~50 (low), with a glycemic load of ~22 per bowl, making it one of the most dietitian-recommended hawker noodle dishes for weight management and metabolic health. The dish is halal and dairy-free. It is regarded as a relatively healthy hawker option due to its lean protein sources, low saturated fat, and moderate sodium compared to other hawker soups. Clinically, the primary concerns are the moderate glycemic load from bee hoon for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, and the sodium content from the anchovy broth for patients with hypertension. Both can be mitigated by reducing the noodle portion and consuming only half the broth. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database with component-level calculation. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes) with component-level cross-validation. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and most micronutrients are verified at confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component analogues at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle data, clearly documented. Overall confidence is HIGH.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite Yong Tau Foo dish (across all 8 item types plus broth and bee hoon) is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-14. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served (including broth absorption by bee hoon and tofu items) is not reported in the HPB database. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore data for this dish preparation). 4. Copper (0.06mg/100g), manganese (0.12mg/100g), and vitamin K (15mcg/100g) values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component analogues (tofu, fish cake, bitter gourd, rice noodles) at confidence 0.9 where HPB data was not available. 5. GI of ~50 is an analogous estimate using rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle per University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by high protein and fat content; direct GI measurement for this composite dish is recommended. 6. GL of ~22 per bowl calculated as (50 × 48g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 24, adjusted to 22 per HPB component estimate for the standard 8-item selection. 7. The dish is halal at hawker stalls that use halal-certified fish paste and crab stick; consumers should verify halal certification at individual stalls. 8. Sodium per bowl (~700mg) is estimated from anchovy broth (~350mg) plus fish paste items (~350mg combined); actual sodium varies by stall and broth concentration.
